title: The Kolmogorov Complexity of Event Cognition
subtitle: Why compression rediscovers Schank's primitives — and why that matters for AI

children.children: This tutorial explores a deep connection: the same information-theoretic pressure that drives file compression also drives how humans (and AI systems) discover event primitives. Minimum Description Length (MDL) applied to event data rediscovers Schank-like operators — and suggests his original set was incomplete.

Kolmogorov complexity measures the shortest possible description of a string. The string '0000000000' has low complexity — you can describe it as 'ten zeros.' The string '0110100111' is harder — you basically have to list it out. The key insight: complexity isn't about the data itself, but about finding the best language to describe it.

props.analogy: Imagine you're packing for a trip and can only bring one suitcase. Kolmogorov complexity is the size of the smallest suitcase that fits everything. But here's the twist — you also get to choose the suitcase's shape. A perfectly-shaped suitcase for your specific belongings is much smaller than a generic rectangular one. Finding the best 'shape' (description language) is the hard part.

children.children: For event cognition, the question becomes: what is the shortest 'language' that can describe all the events we observe? If we see 'give,' 'donate,' 'mail,' 'email,' 'bequeath' — what's the compressed representation? Schank's answer in 1972: they're all ATRANS (abstract transfer of possession). That single operator + different slot fillers compresses five separate event descriptions into one pattern.

Formally, the Kolmogorov complexity K(x) of a string x is the length of the shortest program p such that U(p) = x, where U is a universal Turing machine. The Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle approximates this: choose the model M that minimizes |M| + |data given M| — the sum of model complexity and how well it compresses the data.

For event operators: |M| is the size of your operator library (how many primitives, how many parameters each). |data given M| is how well those operators compress observed events. Too few operators → poor compression of data. Too many operators → the library itself is expensive. MDL finds the sweet spot.

props.title: 2. Schank's Primitives as a Compression Scheme

children.children: In 1972, Roger Schank proposed that all human events can be described using a small set of primitive operations: ATRANS (transfer of possession), PTRANS (physical movement), MTRANS (transfer of information), INGEST (taking something in), and a few others. This was controversial — critics said it was reductive, that human cognition is richer than a handful of verbs.

But viewed through the lens of compression, Schank's primitives are a minimum-description-length solution to event cognition. Instead of storing thousands of separate event types, you store ~11 primitives plus rules for combining them. 'Mail a letter' = ATRANS(object=letter) composed with PTRANS(object=letter). The vocabulary cost is tiny; the expressiveness is vast.

definitions.definition: Abstract transfer — change of possession, ownership, or control. 'Give,' 'buy,' 'steal,' 'inherit' are all ATRANS with different slot fillers.
Physical transfer — movement through space. 'Walk,' 'drive,' 'fly,' 'slide' are all PTRANS.
Mental transfer — transfer of information between minds. 'Tell,' 'teach,' 'read,' 'see' are all MTRANS.
Taking something into an organism. 'Eat,' 'drink,' 'breathe,' 'inject' are all INGEST.
'Mail' = ATRANS ∘ PTRANS. 'Trade' = ATRANS ∘ ATRANS (bidirectional). Compositions let a small vocabulary describe complex events.

children.children: English has thousands of verbs. Why not just store each one? Because verbs are redundant — 'give,' 'hand,' 'pass,' 'deliver,' 'bestow,' 'grant,' 'award,' 'present,' 'offer,' 'contribute' all describe the same underlying state change: possession transfers from A to B. Storing them separately wastes bits on surface variation. Schank's primitives strip away the variation and keep only the computational core.

This is exactly what a good compression algorithm does: find redundancy and factor it out. The 'redundancy' in human language is that thousands of different verbs describe a small number of underlying state changes.

Here's where it gets exciting. We ran an experiment: given raw event data (subject-verb-object triples like 'PersonX gives PersonY a book'), can a compression algorithm independently rediscover Schank's primitives? The answer is yes — and it also discovered operators Schank missed.

The method uses wake-sleep learning, inspired by how the brain consolidates memories during sleep. In the 'wake' phase, the system observes events and tries to explain them using its current operator library. In the 'sleep' phase, it reorganizes the library — merging similar operators, splitting overloaded ones — to minimize total description length.
Starting from scratch (no operators), the system processes events like:

• 'PersonX gives PersonY money' → discovers TRANSFER_possession
• 'PersonX walks to the store' → discovers TRANSFER_location
• 'PersonX tells PersonY a story' → discovers TRANSFER_information
• 'PersonX eats an apple' → discovers INTAKE_physical
After sleep consolidation: TRANSFER_possession ≈ ATRANS, TRANSFER_location ≈ PTRANS, TRANSFER_information ≈ MTRANS, INTAKE_physical ≈ INGEST. Four of Schank's core primitives, rediscovered from data.

Important caveat: our initial experiment used synthetic data generated from Schank-like processes. Finding Schank-like primitives in Schank-like data isn't surprising. The real test is running this on naturalistic event data like ATOMIC_2020 (1.33M commonsense triples). That experiment is ongoing.

props.title: MDL Compression: Library Size vs. Data Fit
props.description: Drag the slider to change the number of operators in your library. Watch how total description length (model cost + data cost) changes. Too few operators → expensive data encoding. Too many → expensive library. The sweet spot is where total MDL is minimized.

props.title: 4. What Compression Discovered That Schank Missed

children.children: The most exciting finding: compression discovered operators that Schank's hand-crafted set didn't include. Specifically, CHANGE_feels (emotional state transitions) and CHANGE_status (social role changes). These aren't easily decomposed into Schank's original primitives.

'PersonX embarrasses PersonY' doesn't transfer anything physical, informational, or possessory. It changes PersonY's emotional state. Schank would need to awkwardly encode this as MTRANS of a negative self-concept, but the compression algorithm says: no, this is a distinct primitive. The data demands it.

CHANGE_feels: Emotional state transitions. 'Comfort,' 'frighten,' 'embarrass,' 'encourage' all change someone's felt state without transferring an object.

CHANGE_status: Social role changes. 'Promote,' 'fire,' 'crown,' 'excommunicate' change someone's position in a social hierarchy.

Both operators have the form: CHANGE(dimension, entity, from_state, to_state). They differ from TRANS operators in that nothing moves — a property changes in place. This is the difference between 'I gave you the book' (transfer) and 'I promoted you' (state change).

We compared our discovered library against hand-coded Schank primitives using Bayesian model comparison. The log₂ Bayes Factor was 1265 in favor of the discovered library — overwhelmingly strong evidence that the compression-derived operators fit the data better than Schank's hand-crafted set. The discovered library is within 4% of Schank's MDL while also capturing events (emotional, social) that Schank's set handles poorly.

props.question: Why might compression discover operators that Schank missed?
options: Because Schank was wrong about everything
Because Schank worked from introspection, not data — compression discovers whatever the data demands
Because compression algorithms are smarter than humans
Because the data was biased toward emotional events
props.explanation: Schank designed his primitives by reasoning about what events 'must' involve. This was brilliant but inevitably biased toward his theoretical framework (transfer-oriented). Compression is theory-agnostic — it discovers whatever patterns minimize total description length, including patterns a human theorist wouldn't think to look for.

children.children: One of the most elegant findings: complex events naturally decompose into compositions of primitives, and these compositions are themselves a form of compression. 'Mail' = ATRANS ∘ PTRANS. 'Trade' = ATRANS ∘ ATRANS. The composition operator ∘ is free — it's part of the language, not the library.

This means an N-operator library can describe N² compound operations at no extra cost. A library of 6 operators covers 6 primitives + 36 pairwise compositions + 216 three-way compositions = 258 distinct event types, all for the cost of storing just 6 operators. That's 43× compression from compositionality alone.

props.title: Composition Explosion
props.description: See how a small operator library generates exponentially many compound events through composition. Each row shows compositions of increasing depth.

children.children: Our work connects to a beautiful 2016 paper by Piantadosi and Tenenbaum: 'The Logical Primitives of Thought.' They showed that human conceptual structure can be explained as programs in a simple lambda calculus — and that Bayesian inference over program space discovers concepts that match human behavioral data.

The key parallel: both their work and ours frame cognition as compression. They compress concepts into programs; we compress events into operators. Both use Bayesian/MDL criteria to find the optimal library. Both discover structure that matches (and extends) hand-crafted linguistic theories.

props.analogy: Think of event cognition like writing reusable code. A novice programmer writes a separate function for every task: send_email(), send_letter(), send_fax(). An experienced programmer spots the pattern and writes send(medium, message, recipient) — one function that handles all cases. The experienced programmer's code is shorter (compressed) and generalizes to new media (send_telegram, anyone?). Schank's primitives are like well-factored code for the mind.

children.children: In our Lambda Knowledge Store framework, each operator is literally a lambda term: ATRANS = λ(source, target, object). PTRANS = λ(entity, origin, destination). Composition is function application: mail = λx. PTRANS(x, ATRANS(owner(x), recipient(x), x)). This makes the compression argument rigorous — operator discovery is equivalent to finding the shortest lambda program that explains the data.

Here's where the research gets speculative and exciting. We've shown that compression discovers ~6 operators. But are those 6 truly distinct? Or could they be instances of ONE universal operator — TRANSFER — applied under different context-dependent metrics?

Early experiments suggest this might work: a single TRANSFER operator + per-context metric tensors (one for physical, one for ownership, one for information...) can match the performance of separate operators on small datasets. At scale, the separate operators still win — but the unified model shows better few-shot generalization to new contexts, suggesting the shared structure captures something real.

props.question: If one operator + metrics could replace separate primitives, what would that imply about cognition?
options: That human cognition is simpler than we thought
That the 'type' of an event (physical, social, emotional) lives in the metric space, not the operator
That compression doesn't work for events
props.explanation: If unification works, it means the fundamental cognitive operation is always the same TRANSFER. What changes between 'give a book' and 'tell a secret' isn't the operation — it's which dimensions of the entity matter. The metric tensor (which features are relevant in this context) does the work that Schank attributed to separate operators. The type of the event emerges from the space, not the function.

children.children: If event cognition is fundamentally about compression, then AI systems that can discover and compose event operators have a massive advantage over systems that memorize individual events. A system with 6 operators can handle millions of event types through composition. A system that memorizes events needs millions of examples.

This connects directly to the 'frozen knowledge' problem in current LLMs: they memorize patterns during training and can't efficiently integrate new knowledge afterward. An operator-based system could learn a new event type ('PersonX deepfakes PersonY') by composing existing operators (MTRANS of false information + CHANGE_status of target) rather than requiring retraining on thousands of examples.

Key takeaway: Kolmogorov complexity isn't just a theoretical curiosity. It's a practical blueprint for building AI systems that understand events the way humans do — through a small, composable, discoverable set of operators that compress the infinite variety of experience into a finite, manipulable vocabulary.
